artificial-intelligence/embeddings/setti.title: Setting up and populating a vector database
artificial-intelligence/embeddings/setti.description:
This note is gonna go over how to set up and populating a local vector database of choice, which is gonna be qdrant. I'd imagine the setup process for most other vectordb services like milvus is pretty similar.Getting a container running in docker is pretty trivial so I'm gonna skip this. All that needs to be done is running docker run -p 6333:6333 qdrant/qdrant, which will pull & run the official qdrant image and expose port 6333 to the host. This gives us access to the web-dashboard at http://localhost:6333/dashboard. The next step would be to choose an embedding model that is used to turn strings into vectors.
Some background on it is covered in The magic of word embeddings.
A pretty solid and resource-light model would be nomic-embed-text... At least it was the perfect balance of context window, embedding quality and hardware requirements back when it released 2 years ago. Filtering by "Embedding" on ollama.com also shows that it is still quite popular:
Anyways, after ollama pull nomic-embed-text-ing the embedding model, it is time to take a look at the most important matter, the data.Models matter Trying out different models will definitely give you different results every time, so I'd recommend testing with 2 or 3 different ones. I'd probably go with qwen3-embedding and embeddinggemma. If you want multilingual embeddings, try nomic-embed-text-v2-moe.
Back when I built a semantic search component for a university project, the class was given a subset of the wikidata knowledge graph as data source, but you can of course take any dataset you like. So the next step now is to create a python script that pulls out the relevant data from the knowledge graph, embeds the texts, generates an ID for each embedding (qdrant requires this) and then pushes it to qdrant.But first, install all the required python packages for doing this: pip install SPARQLWrapper qdrant-client requests. Of course, if you don't use knowledge graphs, omit SPARQLWrapperfrom the list.Next is the python code to import packages and define some constants like the embedding api endpoint and SPARQL prefixes to make retrieval easier:import requests from SPARQLWrapper import SPARQLWrapper, JSON from qdrant_client import QdrantClient from qdrant_client.http import models import hashlib print("Starting embedding loader...") OLLAMA_URL = "http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings" PREFIX = """ PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> PREFIX : <http://h-da.de/fbi/art/> PREFIX owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> PREFIX xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/> BASE <http://h-da.de/fbi/art/> """ sparql = SPARQLWrapper("http://fuseki:3030/art-dataset/query") # check if the db already exists client = QdrantClient(url="http://qdrant:6333") if client.collection_exists("artists"): print("Collection 'artists' already exists") exit(1) #client.delete_collection("artists") collection_created = False batch_size = 100 offset = 0 total_inserted = 0 sparql again is just related to the data loading and points to the SPARQL endpoint to retreive data from. The client holds the reference to the qdrant server. Depending on how your docker setup looks like, you will need to change this url to whatever you set up.Anyways, after getting the client reference, the first thing I do is check if the "collection" (vector db term for a "table") called "artists" already exists. Because why would we want to embed all of the data again when starting up the program?Lastly are some other variables I'll need later in the big loop where all of the data gets embedded and pushed.After the constants, I defined some global methods to get the embeddings from the model and to assign one piece of embedded information an id:def get_embedding(text): r = requests.post( OLLAMA_URL, # the endpoint defined above json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": text}, # the model timeout=30 ) r.raise_for_status() return r.json()["embedding"] # only return embedding def make_id(uri): # Artist IDs are provided as URIs in Wikidata, so we hash it to get an integer ID return int(hashlib.md5(uri.encode()).hexdigest()[:8], 16) You will probably need to adjust the make_id function for your data - or keep it if you wanna hash whatever data you provide.This is where all of the data gets processed by the steps mentioned earlier: Load a batch of data from knowledge graph using SPARQL Embed & generate IDs If the current batch is the first batch (the collection "artists" doesn't exist already), create a collection called "artists" Upsert the embedded data to qdrant I put the whole loop-code below and gonna go over the code equivalents of the steps above. Keep in mind that around all of the code is one giant while True:.Step 1: loading batched data from the datasourcesparql.setQuery(PREFIX + f""" SELECT ?artist ?label ?desc WHERE {{ ?artist a :person ; rdfs:label ?label . OPTIONAL {{ ?artist :description ?desc . }} }} LIMIT {batch_size} OFFSET {offset} """) sparql.setReturnFormat(JSON) results = sparql.query().convert() artists = [ { "id": r["artist"]["value"], "name": r["label"]["value"], "desc": r.get("desc", {}).get("value", "") } for r in results.get("results", {}).get("bindings", []) #type:ignore ] if not artists: print(f"No more artists to fetch at offset {offset}") break print(f"Fetched {len(artists)} artists (offset: {offset})") As you can see above, there's a bunch of query magic happening to the SPARQL endpoint, but what it does is just fetch batches of data (id, name and description) for each artist and turns it into a list of dictionaries with the respective keys.Step 2: embedding and generating IDsvectors = [] payloads = [] ids = [] for artist in artists: text = f"{artist['name']}. {artist['desc']}" # prepare text for embedding emb = get_embedding(text) vectors.append(emb) ids.append(make_id(artist["id"])) payloads.append({ "wikidataID": artist["id"], "name": artist["name"] }) print(f"Generated {len(vectors)} embeddings") Iterating through the artists list from step 1, I concatenate the artists' name and description into one long string and pass it to the get_embedding function that requests the embedding model to turn that text into a vector. I then populate a list vectors with these results. Alongside, I populate the lists ids, which contains the ids of the artists and payloads which holds additional information about the artist.Important note regarding the lists Please keep in mind that all of these three lists MUST be the same length, so keeping care of them alongside each other is VERY dangerous. I'd recommend packing all of the information into one container (like a dataclass?) and only keeping one list, which makes it easier to guarantee the same amount of data! Step 3: Assuring the collection exists Remember that variable collection_createdfrom the first code snippet at the top? Yeah, that's gonna come into play now:if not collection_created: vec_dims = len(vectors[0]) print(f"Vector dimension: {vec_dims}") client.create_collection( collection_name="artists", vectors_config=models.VectorParams( size=vec_dims, distance=models.Distance.COSINE ) ) collection_created = True If the collection already exists, we exit the code earlier, but now, if the collection doesn't exist AND we're inside the loop, we wanna take the first probe of vectors to use as configuration data for the collection.The most important information to create a collection is the dimensions of the vectors. All vectors must have this exact size, for example a common size would be 768 dimensions. So, I get the vec_dims and pass them to the config, next to the distance parameter, which distance function is used to determine the similarity of two vectors.
In this case, I opted for cosine similarity. Other functions include dot, euclid and manhattan, each with their own use cases' pros and cons.The main reason to use cosine similarity is that I want similar artists to be in close proximity (in vector space) even though they may use different wordings in their description.Step 4: Pushing data to qdrant Almost done! The last step is just pushing data to the database:client.upsert( collection_name="artists", points=models.Batch( ids=ids, vectors=vectors, payloads=payloads ) ) total_inserted += len(artists) print(f"Inserted batch into Qdrant. Total: {total_inserted} artists\n") offset += batch_size Using the qdrant client upsert function, I push a batch of ids, vectors & payloads to the database. These are the same lists from step 2, and yes, they must be the same length. Then finally, do some keeping track of offsets for batching and we're done!You'll be able to look at your collection through the web dashboard!
Thanks for reading! Back to the Artificial Intelligence Root.For the sake of context, I put the complete pipeline code below:import requests from SPARQLWrapper import SPARQLWrapper, JSON from qdrant_client import QdrantClient from qdrant_client.http import models import hashlib OLLAMA_URL = "http://llm-service:11434/api/embeddings" PREFIX = """ PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> PREFIX : <http://h-da.de/fbi/art/> PREFIX owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> PREFIX xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/> BASE <http://h-da.de/fbi/art/> """ def get_embedding(text): r = requests.post( OLLAMA_URL, json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": text}, timeout=30 ) r.raise_for_status() return r.json()["embedding"] def make_id(uri): return int(hashlib.md5(uri.encode()).hexdigest()[:8], 16) sparql = SPARQLWrapper("http://fuseki:3030/art-dataset/query") # check if the db already exists client = QdrantClient(url="http://qdrant:6333") if client.collection_exists("artists"): print("Collection 'artists' already exists") exit(1) #client.delete_collection("artists") collection_created = False batch_size = 10 offset = 0 total_inserted = 0 while True: # step 1 sparql.setQuery(PREFIX + f""" SELECT ?artist ?label ?desc WHERE {{ ?artist a :person ; rdfs:label ?label . OPTIONAL {{ ?artist :description ?desc . }} }} LIMIT {batch_size} OFFSET {offset} """) sparql.setReturnFormat(JSON) results = sparql.query().convert() artists = [ { "id": r["artist"]["value"], "name": r["label"]["value"], "desc": r.get("desc", {}).get("value", "") } for r in results.get("results", {}).get("bindings", []) #type:ignore ] if not artists: print(f"No more artists to fetch at offset {offset}") break print(f"Fetched {len(artists)} artists (offset: {offset})") # embed current batch vectors = [] payloads = [] ids = [] # step 2 for artist in artists: text = f"{artist['name']}. {artist['desc']}" # prepare text for embedding emb = get_embedding(text) vectors.append(emb) ids.append(make_id(artist["id"])) payloads.append({ "wikidataID": artist["id"], "name": artist["name"] }) print(f"Generated {len(vectors)} embeddings") # step 3 # if its the first batch , we create a collection on qdrant server if not collection_created: collection_size = len(vectors[0]) print(f"Vector dimension: {collection_size}") client.create_collection( collection_name="artists", vectors_config=models.VectorParams( size=collection_size, distance=models.Distance.COSINE ) ) collection_created = True #step 4 # push batch to qdrant client.upsert( collection_name="artists", points=models.Batch( ids=ids, vectors=vectors, payloads=payloads ) ) total_inserted += len(artists) print(f"Inserted batch into Qdrant. Total: {total_inserted} artists\n") offset += batch_size
Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026

artificial-intelligence/embeddings/the-m.title: The magic of word embeddings
artificial-intelligence/embeddings/the-m.description:
When I came to first learn about word embeddings through the interesting lecture held by Prof. Bernhard Humm during my Master's course, I was shocked at how simple but yet effective the whole concept of turning strings of characters into n-dimensional vectors. Abstracting away all the mathematical details, let me show you things that are possible to do with this.Given a word (or a whole text), an embedding model turns the word(s) into one high dimensional vector of numbers, while keeping the semantic relationship in mind. To visualize:
As you can see, cat, duck and dog are all close to each other in vectorspace, as their semantic relation is closely related: They are all animals. Same goes for C# and C++ at the bottom, they are both programming languages. The origin point does not have a meaning but is instead just mathematically arbitrary.Now let's take a look at the useful feature, which is arithmetics:
As seen in the image, man & woman as well as king & queen are aligned in one plane, and share the same distance to each other respectively.One major use-case of word embeddings is semantic search. Assuming you have a populated vector database of maaaaany vectors created by turning whole webpages into embeddings, a search engine could do the following:
The search engine turns the user query into an embedding vector and shoots it into the vectorspace. To get the results, the most close points in the vectorspace are returned. For , this would be "orange" and "lime" in the example.That's it for embeddings in this article. If you want to learn more about topics like this, consider getting the book "Applied Artificial Intelligence" by Prof. Humm for free (or a voluntary donation) at leanpub.com. I am not affiliated with or endorsed to advertise the book by Prof. Humm but genuinely think it's a very well and easily understandable view into the world of AI, without looking at the complicated behind the scenes maths for too long.
Back to the Artificial Intelligence Root.
Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026
artificial-intelligence/embeddings/the-m.sourcePath: Artificial Intelligence/Embeddings/The magic of word embeddings.md

artificial-intelligence/machine-learning.title: Training an Ordinary Least Squares-based regression model
artificial-intelligence/machine-learning.description:
In this note, I'm gonna quickly go over how I managed to train a regression model that yielded that got placed in the top 200 for a kaggle challenge!Firstly, I wanna clarify that the intro sounds a bit over the top and I have to admit that it is. The challenge that I took part in was from 2016 and closed the "official", prize winning submissions 11 years ago. But nevertheless, I think it might be interesting to cover the approach I used to land a good score for the solution.The first (zeroth) step should always be to make yourself known with the data. Take a look at the dataset, the format, the scale, the encoding, whatever. The background of the given data are restaurants with different information about them, and the challenge is to create a prediction (regression) model to predict the revenue based on the given metadata.Id,Open Date,City,CityGroup,Type,P1,P2,P3,P4,...,P34,P35,P36,P37,revenue 0,01/22/2011,Niğde,Other,FC,1,4.0,4.0,4.0,1,2,5,4,...,0,0,0,0,0,0,5653753.0 Above is an excerpt of the data given. As you can see, there's one row for Id, which is just a sequentially counting index for each row, then a date, some string, another string, and.. another string. Additionally, there are some obscure P1 to P37 values that nobody knows where they come from. Lastly, there's the "revenue" field, which is gonna be the target value for our prediction model.So, the first real step should be to create what's known as a correlation matrix. The correlation matrix tells us how different values correlate (change) in respect to a target value - in this case the revenue.But before going ahead and generating this matrix, we should first look at the data scales to answer the following questions: How many different Cities are there? How many different CityGroups are there? How many different Types are there? All of these matter because models can't be trained on just the strings, instead we wanna create numerical mappings to represent these different classes.So, after loading the data to dataframes, let's normalize the data:def preprocess_data_compact(train, test): mappings = {} # City train['City'], uniques = pd.factorize(train['City']) mappings['City'] = dict(zip(uniques, range(len(uniques)))) test['City'] = test['City'].map(mappings['City']).fillna(-1).astype(int) # City Group train['City Group'], uniques = pd.factorize(train['City Group']) mappings['City Group'] = dict(zip(uniques, range(len(uniques)))) test['City Group'] = test['City Group'].map(mappings['City Group']).fillna(-1).astype(int) # Type train['Type'], uniques = pd.factorize(train['Type']) mappings['Type'] = dict(zip(uniques, range(len(uniques)))) test['Type'] = test['Type'].map(mappings['Type']).fillna(-1).astype(int) # --- Handle dates --- for df, is_train in [(train, True), (test, False)]: df['Open Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Open Date'], format='%m/%d/%Y') df['OpenYear'] = df['Open Date'].dt.year df['OpenMonth'] = df['Open Date'].dt.month df['OpenDay'] = df['Open Date'].dt.day # Drop Id only from train drop_cols = ['Open Date'] if is_train: drop_cols.append('Id') df.drop(columns=drop_cols, inplace=True, errors='ignore') # Convert to numeric just in case df[:] = df.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='ignore') # Optional: print mappings for debugging for col, mapping in mappings.items(): print(f"{col} mapping:", mapping) return train, test df_train, df_test = pp.preprocess_data_compact(df_train, df_test) # --- Normalize numeric columns (Z-score scaling) # (Skip target column 'revenue') num_cols = df_train.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.drop('revenue') mean = df_train[num_cols].mean() std = df_train[num_cols].std() df_train[num_cols] = (df_train[num_cols] - mean) / std df_test[num_cols] = (df_test[num_cols] - mean) / std Above code does a couple of things and looks more complicated than it is.The preprocess_data_compact function first creates mappings for our string fields "City", "City Group" and "Type" for the reason explained earlier:City mapping: {'İstanbul': 0, 'Ankara': 1, 'Diyarbakır': 2, 'Tokat': 3, 'Gaziantep': 4, 'Afyonkarahisar': 5, 'Edirne': 6, 'Kocaeli': 7, 'Bursa': 8, 'İzmir': 9, 'Sakarya': 10, 'Elazığ': 11, 'Kayseri': 12, 'Eskişehir': 13, 'Şanlıurfa': 14, 'Samsun': 15, 'Adana': 16, 'Antalya': 17, 'Kastamonu': 18, 'Uşak': 19, 'Muğla': 20, 'Kırklareli': 21, 'Konya': 22, 'Karabük': 23, 'Tekirdağ': 24, 'Denizli': 25, 'Balıkesir': 26, 'Aydın': 27, 'Amasya': 28, 'Kütahya': 29, 'Bolu': 30, 'Trabzon': 31, 'Isparta': 32, 'Osmaniye': 33} City Group mapping: {'Big Cities': 0, 'Other': 1} Type mapping: {'IL': 0, 'FC': 1, 'DT': 2} So now we've got integer values representing these strings. Nice. Next, we handle the date values by first parsing the string representation and then disassembling it as fine-grained as possible, creating "OpenYear", "OpenMonth" and "OpenDay".The thought behind this is, that maybe there's a strong correlation between what month restaurants open... Maybe more people go to restaurants that opened in Summer months?Lastly, the mean() and std() functions normalize the values of all numeric columns to a range of -1 to 1. This is called "Standardization" and it handles far outliers much better than just "min-max-scaling" as large outliers don't skew the scale as much.Anyways now that all the data is prepared and normalized, we can finally generate a correlation matrix:import seaborn as sns import matplotlib.pyplot as plt numeric_cols = df_train.select_dtypes(include=['number']) # only numeric corr_matrix = numeric_cols.corr() # Plot heatmap plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10)) sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=False, cmap='coolwarm', linewidths=0.3) plt.title("Correlation Heatmap", fontsize=16) plt.show() Above code is pretty straight forward: we only take numeric values and then render the map:
Alongside with this, we also generate a nice plot that may be easier to read:revenue_corr = numeric_cols.corr()['revenue'].drop('revenue').sort_values(ascending=False) print(revenue_corr) # Visualize plt.figure(figsize=(8, 12)) sns.barplot(x=revenue_corr, y=revenue_corr.index, palette='coolwarm') plt.title("Feature Correlation with Revenue (excluding revenue)", fontsize=14) plt.xlabel("Correlation with Revenue") plt.ylabel("Feature") plt.tight_layout()
So these two tell us the following: the more saturated the color, the stronger the positive or negative the correlation of the given column is in regards to the revenue.As you can see, "OpenYear", "City Group", "City" (these last two also strongly correlate, of course), "P29" and "P13" have a considerably strong negative correlation with revenue. On the other hand, "P2", "P28" and "P6" have a strong positive correlation with revenue.Choosing the predictors There are other attributes that also show strong correlation, and to be honest its up to you what to choose (start by experimenting with the top X strongest ones) but I elected these as the "most influential" attributes to train my regression model on. So with this set, training the model is simple enough:import statsmodels.api as sm # Select predictors and target X = df_train[['OpenYear', 'City Group', 'City', "P29", "P13", "P6", "P28", "P2"]] y = df_train['revenue'] X = sm.add_constant(X) # Fit OLS regression model model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit() print(model.summary()) In above code, we define the predictors and target and add a constant, which adds a column containing only 1.0s. This is necessary for the model to calculate the intercept, the mathematical origin of the regression-fit. Then define the type of regression model, in this case ordinary least squares, and finally fit it.Printing the model summary shows a whole lot of stats about the model, for example the F-statistic, which represents "the probability, that the correlation between revenue and features is pure coincidence".This value is 0.00229, so way underneath the 0.05 mark which is great.Finally, after doing the same things with the test data and exporting the results to a nice csv file, we're done!
Back to the Index?
Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026

legal/privacy.html.title: Privacy
legal/privacy.html.description:
Diese Website wird als statische Website über GitHub Pages bereitgestellt. Zur sicheren und performanten Auslieferung der Inhalte wird zudem das Content Delivery Network (CDN) sowie weitere Sicherheits- und Routing-Dienste von Cloudflare genutzt.Beim Aufruf dieser Website werden aus technischen Gründen bestimmte Informationen verarbeitet. Hierzu können insbesondere die IP-Adresse, Datum und Uhrzeit des Zugriffs, angeforderte Ressourcen, Informationen über den verwendeten Browser und das Betriebssystem sowie weitere technisch erforderliche Verbindungsdaten gehören. Die Verarbeitung erfolgt insbesondere zur Bereitstellung der Website, zur Gewährleistung der Systemsicherheit, zur Abwehr von Missbrauchs- und Angriffsversuchen sowie zur Optimierung der Auslieferung der Inhalte.Im Rahmen dieser technischen Bereitstellung können personenbezogene Daten durch die eingesetzten Dienstleister verarbeitet werden. Weitere Informationen zur Datenverarbeitung durch GitHub finden sich unter: https://docs.github.com/de/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-general-privacy-statementInformationen zur Datenverarbeitung durch Cloudflare sind verfügbar unter:
https://www.cloudflare.com/privacypolicy/Die Verarbeitung erfolgt auf Grundlage von Art. 6 Abs. 1 lit. f DSGVO. Das berechtigte Interesse besteht in der sicheren, stabilen und effizienten Bereitstellung dieser Website.

my-favorites/my-favorite-obsidian-plugin.title: My favorite Obsidian plugins
my-favorites/my-favorite-obsidian-plugin.description:
This note presents my favorite plugins for Obsidian! I "learned" using Obsidian during my studies and primarily took lecture notes, so keep in mind the plugins I introduce here were especially useful for this use-case.I like to automate (even taking notes!) a lot and you'll see why after reading my top 5!The probably most useful plugin when it comes to create structured and coherent looking notes. Templater allows you to define a directory of markdown files to use as a base when creating new notes, with pre-filled content or even with dynamically generated text. But even moving files around can be done, as it allows to call Obsidians JS api.I used it during university and now at work to enforce an automatic directory structure, by implementing a pop-up that asks me to input the project or class that the new note should belong to, and automatically move it into the right directory then.It also assigned the corresponding tags and set the current date and time as properties of the note, ready to be used with the Dataview plugin which I'll get to now.Everyone loves SQL and querying. Who doesn't like organized data??!Dataview is the defined standard when it comes to automatically generating and updating my Dashboard. It allows you to write SQL-Like syntax to query notes from different directories, filter by tags, name, date, time, whatever. It's one of those plugins you can sink hours into perfecting the use of. The great part is, even little effort and knowledge yields useful results!Even today's LLMs like ChatGPT can generate queries for you...An example use would be the content list on my Artificial Intelligence Root note, that lists all of the notes with the #ai tag in the Artificial Intelligence directory.The query to create that table looks like this:TABLE file.folder AS Directory, file.mday AS Edited FROM "Artificial Intelligence" WHERE contains(file.tags, "#ai") As you can see, quite a simple query, and to be honest even more complicated than it needs to be, as all of the notes in the AI directory should also have the AI tag.This plugin does what the name suggests: It enhances the pdf export for notes.I've found myself struggling to get nice looking exports for a long time, cause either images were off-center, bullet points didn't look right, or whatever.Better Export Pdf fixes these things by allowing you to define a custom css snippet to apply to the file before export. There are so many settings to configure, you can take a look around for yourself.
I published the "pseudo scientific look" snippet I used here.This plugin is the whole reason why sam-ko.eu looks the way it does. A very well made web export plugin that allows you to choose between "Local Website", "Online Website" and some other fancy targets. Most importantly does it provide support for navigation bars, the graph view, filtering by tags in the searchbar and maaaany other settings you can configure for your website. Of course it also allows you to prepend your own html or css to add features that aren't already included.Image converter primarily does what the title suggests: It allows you to convert / compress / transform images on-paste! It also moves them to a specified directory, renames images with timestamp support and most importantly: Allows you to add image captions! It also adds alignment support for images like center, left, right and top.. you know the drill...This one also definitely adds to the "keeping my vault clean and structured"! mindset!This one isn't deep, better tables adds quality of life to markdown tables by allowing you to expand rows and columns more easily, adds excel like features like formulas and formatting styles for table content and exporting to .csv .I already wanted to finish this article but then realized I am missing the Kanban plugin, which I just discovered some days ago. For everyone who loves structuring their tasks with a Kanban board, this one is a must have and feels like a native part of Obsidian. Pin your Kanban tab and get to work!
Back to the Index?
Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026

about-me.html.title: About me
about-me.html.description:
Hey, I'm Samuel!I'm a computer scientist from Germany. Although CS is a pretty interesting domain, there's no need to get into details here, so I'm gonna keep it short:My interests cover lower level applications such as embedded systems and game modding, but also machine learning, data science, computer vision and completely unrelated- desktop development.Outside of tech, I'm a huge fan of call ducks.My music taste ranges from drum'n'bass all the way to what some describe as "construction site noise".Also I love travelling around Europe by train and exploring further away regions.My best personal experience in travelling was probably two exchange semesters in South Korean universities.Africa and South America also seem very interesting and beautiful to me but I haven't gotten around to visit there.
If you want to contact me, you can either send me an e-mail to the address given in the Imprint or use my archaic looking DIY solution I built here. For any legal matters I suggest the first, for just saying hello I recommend the latter.
Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026

index.html.title: Index
index.html.description:
This is my personal website, come take a look around.To create this website, I've used Obsidian with the awesome Export Webpage Plugin. The most prominent and useful features are probably the nice navigation bar and the graph view, which allows you to navigate between documents by their link-relationship. Also the small tree icon changes the scope of displayed nodes: Global mode shows all documents Local mode only shows directly connected documents
For now I recommend you to take a look at my notes about AI and My favorite Obsidian plugins.

Imprint - sam-ko.eu 2026

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
